_doc: Hand-written expected answers per prompt. Used by run_prompts.py to compose training pairs. A prompt without an entry here gets `expected: null` in the saved JSON — fill in manually before running the trainer.
ARO snippet for a Mustache-style templat:
```aro
(Render: Example) {
    Create the <template> with "Hello, {{name}}!".
    Create the <data> with { name: "Ada" }.
    Render the <html> from the <template> with <data>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <html>.
}
```
ARO snippet that demonstrates lazy / str:
Every action is lazy by default — chained Compute/Reduce fuse automatically.

```aro
(StreamingPipeline: Example) {
    Create the <numbers> with [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
    Compute the <sorted: sort> from <numbers>.
    Reduce the <total: Integer> from the <sorted> with sum().
    Return an <OK: status> with <total>.
}
```
```aro
(Intersect: Example) {
    Create the <a> with [1, 2, 3].
    Create the <b> with [2, 3, 4].
    Compute the <common: intersect> from <a> with <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <common>.
}
```
ARO snippet that uses a state machine to:
```aro
(Advance Order: Orders) {
    Extract the <order> from the <input>.
    Accept the <transition> for the <order> with "shipped".
    Return an <OK: status> with <order>.
}
```
ARO snippet that uses the date system ob:
```aro
(Today: Example) {
    Create the <today> with the <date: today>.
    Log <today> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <today>.
}
```
Add error handling (Throw) to this featu: Add a `Throw a <BadRequest: status> with "..." when <field> == "".` guard near the top so invalid input rejects before any work runs.
Add `Log <variable> to the <console>.` before the `Return` so the value is visible at runtime without changing the return.
Are there any deprecated actions I shoul: Avoid the old eager-execution `Force` and the `ARO_LAZY_ACTIONS` flag — both removed in 0.10. `aro lm` is also gone; use `aro ask` instead.
Best practice: how do I test an event ha: Use `aro test` with colocated tests (ARO-0015). Given a `event-name.given.yaml` payload, the test framework fires the event into the handler and asserts the resulting state via `then.yaml`. No HTTP server needed.
Best practice: naming convention for rep: Hyphenated lowercase singular-noun + `-repository` (or `-store` for read-only seed data): `user-repository`, `order-repository`, `products.store`. Match the entity name to keep handler events readable (`<event: user>` reads naturally next to `<user-repository>`).
Best practice: when should I split a fea: When two concerns aren't sharing data. Pass control via an event (`Emit`) or a published variable. A 30-line feature set that does "validate, persist, notify" usually wants to be `(Save X) → emits XCreated → (Notify) handles XCreated`.
Best practice: where should I put my ope: In the application root, next to `main.aro`. The runtime looks there first; subdirectories under `sources/` aren't scanned for it.
Code review: what's wrong stylistically: Three style issues: `do_stuff` should be `Do Stuff` (Title Case for feature-set names), the activity `x` should be a Title-Cased noun phrase, and the result `X` needs angle brackets and hyphenated lowercase. Fixed: `(Do Stuff: Example) { Compute the <x> from <a> + <b>. Return an <OK: status> with <x>. }`
Compare `Emit` and `Publish` — when do I: `Emit` produces a one-shot event that fires handler feature sets. `Publish` exposes a named variable across feature sets in the same application — other code can `Extract` from the published name without an event round-trip. Emit for asynchronous fan-out; Publish for shared state.
Compare `For-each` with `When` — are the: No. `for each` iterates over a collection; `when` gates a single statement on a condition. You can combine: `for each <x> in <xs> where <x: active> is true { ... }` iterates only matching elements.
Compare `Stage` and `Commit` in the git: `Stage` is OWN-role: it adds files to the git index. `Commit` is EXPORT-role: it writes the staged tree as a new commit and emits a `GitCommit` event. You stage to choose what goes in, you commit to write it.
Convert a Go HTTP handler that returns u:
```aro
(getUser: Users API) {
    Extract the <id> from the <pathParameters: id>.
    Retrieve the <user> from the <user-repository> where <id> is <id>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <user>.
}
```
Convert this synchronous file read into: Lazy is already the default in ARO ≥0.10. Just write the Read normally; the runtime returns a future and forces it only when something reads the value.
Diagnose the parser error: \"Expected pr: The `+` operator only lives inside `Compute` expressions. The parser hit `+` where it expected one of the action prepositions (from/to/with/for/into/at). Likely your `Compute the <x> from <a> + <b>.` is missing the `from`, or you wrote it as `Compute the <x> + <b>` directly. Use: `Compute the <x> from <a> + <b>.`
Diagnose: \"Variables are immutable — ca: ARO bindings are immutable. You assigned to `<count>` twice. Rename the second binding: `Create the <updated-count> with <count> + 1.`
Does ARO have a type system?: Yes, but it's mostly inferred. Types appear in qualifiers (`<count: Integer>`, `<avg: Float>`) and OpenAPI schemas. Most code is untyped strings + numbers + objects + lists; the compiler enforces structural correctness rather than nominal types.
Does ARO support concurrency?: Yes. Actions execute lazily and overlap by default — independent results run in parallel on a dedicated `ActionTaskExecutor`. Effects (Log, Store, Emit, Commit) keep source order. `parallel for each` runs loop iterations concurrently with a configurable cap.
ELI5: explain the \"code is the error me: Write only the happy path. When something goes wrong at runtime, the runtime turns the failing statement into the error message verbatim: "Cannot retrieve the user from the user-repository where id = 530". The error reads like the code; no separate exception class to look up.
ELI5: what's a feature set?: A small, named piece of code. It has a name (`Hello World`), a category (`Application-Start`, `User API`, `OrderCreated Handler`), and a body of action statements. The runtime fires it when the matching event happens (the app starts, an HTTP request arrives, an event is emitted).
ELI5: why are ARO variables immutable?: Each name binds to one value forever. To change a value you make a new binding with a new name (`<updated-count>`). That way you can never accidentally read the old value somewhere else — and the runtime can safely run independent statements in parallel.
`Compute` is the OWN-role action that derives a new immutable value from existing data. It takes a source and an operation: arithmetic (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`), a qualifier (`length`, `uppercase`, `lowercase`, `hash`, `sort`, `reverse`, `union`, `intersect`, `difference`), or string concat (`++`). Result is bound to the angle-bracketed name on the left.
Explain the difference between Extract a: `Extract` is OWN-role: it pulls a field out of an in-memory object (`<user: name>`) or a structured input (`<request: body>`). `Retrieve` is REQUEST-role: it fetches a record from an external source — usually a repository, sometimes a service — by id or query.
Explain why this fails aro check: `Retur: `Return` requires an article (`an`/`a`/`the`) before the result. Fixed: `Return an <OK: status> for the <action>.` Every result and object needs an article in ARO.
Find the bug in this code: `(Sum: Exampl:
`Compute` uses `from`, not `as`. Also `<a>` and `<b>` need to be Created first. Fixed:

```aro
(Sum: Example) {
    Create the <a> with 3.
    Create the <b> with 5.
    Compute the <sum> from <a> + <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <sum>.
}
```
Find the bug: `(Greet: Example) { Log \":
Two bugs: `<console>` needs the article `the` (`to the <console>`), and `Return` needs a value (`Return an <OK: status> for the <log>`). Fixed:

```aro
(Greet: Example) {
    Log "Hi" to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <log>.
}
```
Find the bug: `(getUser: Users API) { Ex:
Dot notation isn't allowed inside angle brackets. Use the qualifier form: `<pathParameters: id>`. Fixed:

```aro
(getUser: Users API) {
    Extract the <id> from the <pathParameters: id>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <id>.
}
```
Generate the feature set names I need fr: Same — paste the openapi.yaml. The feature-set name for each route is the `operationId` value verbatim, with the surrounding API name from the spec title.
Generate the operationId handlers for th: I need the openapi.yaml content to generate the handlers. Paste it and I'll write one feature set per `operationId`, named to match.
Give me an example of a Compute action t:
```aro
(AddTwo: Example) {
    Create the <a> with 7.
    Create the <b> with 5.
    Compute the <sum> from <a> + <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <sum>.
}
```
Sure — what do you want help with? A feature set, an HTTP route, an event handler, a repository, debugging an error? Paste the prompt or the code and I'll work through it.
I write and explain ARO code. Try: `aro ask "write a hello world application"` or `aro ask "how do I extract a path parameter"`. Slash commands: `/help`, `/fix <path>`, `/explain <path>`, `/openapi`.
Hi. I'm an ARO coding assistant. Tell me what you'd like to build — a feature set, an HTTP route, a repository observer — and I'll write it for you.
How to accept a state transition?:
Use the `Accept` action against the entity and the target state.

```aro
(Ship Order: Orders) {
    Extract the <order> from the <input>.
    Accept the <transition> for the <order> with "shipped".
    Return an <OK: status> with <order>.
}
```
How to build an event-driven order flow:
## openapi.yaml
```yaml
openapi: 3.0.3
info: { title: Orders, version: 1.0.0 }
paths:
  /orders:
    post: { operationId: createOrder }
```

## main.aro
```aro
(Application-Start: Orders) {
    Start the <http-server> with {}.
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}

(createOrder: Orders) {
    Extract the <order> from the <request: body>.
    Store the <order> into the <order-repository>.
    Emit an <OrderPlaced: event> with <order>.
    Return a <Created: status> with <order>.
}
```

## handlers.aro
```aro
(Send Confirmation: OrderPlaced Handler) {
    Extract the <order> from the <event: order>.
    Send the <confirmation> to the <order: email>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <notification>.
}

(Adjust Inventory: OrderPlaced Handler) {
    Extract the <order> from the <event: order>.
    Retrieve the <item> from the <inventory-repository> where <id> is <order>.
    Store the <item> into the <inventory-repository>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <adjustment>.
}
```
How to call a plugin action like Markdow:
Plugin actions look like `Handle.Verb` — same call-site shape as built-in actions, just namespaced.

```aro
(RenderMarkdown: Example) {
    Create the <md> with "# Title".
    Markdown.ToHTML the <html> from <md>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <html>.
}
```
How to call a plugin action?:
Plugin actions are `Handle.Verb` calls.

```aro
(RenderMarkdown: Example) {
    Create the <md> with "# Title".
    Markdown.ToHTML the <html> from <md>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <html>.
}
```
How to chain transformations on a list?:
Multiple Compute/Reduce calls in sequence — the runtime fuses them automatically.

```aro
(ChainTransforms: Example) {
    Create the <values> with [10, 20, 30].
    Compute the <scaled: sort> from <values>.
    Reduce the <total: Integer> from the <scaled> with sum().
    Return an <OK: status> with <total>.
}
```
How to check if a file exists?:
Use the `Exists` action.

```aro
(FileExists: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "data.json".
    Exists the <present> from the <file: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <present>.
}
```
How to check if a number is greater than:
Use a `when` guard.

```aro
(IsGreater: Example) {
    Create the <a> with 10.
    Create the <b> with 5.
    Log "a is greater" to the <console> when <a> > <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <check>.
}
```
How to commit changes via the git system:
Use `Commit ... to the <git>`.

```aro
(Commit Work: Example) {
    Commit the <result> to the <git> with "chore: update".
    Return an <OK: status> with <result>.
}
```
How to compare two values?:
Use the `Compare` action.

```aro
(CompareTwo: Example) {
    Create the <left> with 10.
    Create the <right> with 15.
    Compare the <result> against <left> with <right>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <result>.
}
```
How to compose multiple actions in a pip:
ARO supports an implicit pipeline: chain Compute/Filter/Reduce on a list and the runtime fuses them.

```aro
(Pipeline: Example) {
    Create the <numbers> with [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
    Compute the <doubled> from <numbers>.
    Reduce the <total: Integer> from the <doubled> with sum().
    Return an <OK: status> with <total>.
}
```
How to compute a date in the future?:
Add a duration to a date via `Compute` with `+`.

```aro
(FutureDate: Example) {
    Create the <today> with the <date: today>.
    Compute the <next-week> from <today> + "7 days".
    Return an <OK: status> with <next-week>.
}
```
How to compute the absolute value of a n:
Use a `when` guard on the sign.

```aro
(Abs: Example) {
    Create the <n> with -5.
    Compute the <abs> from <n>.
    Compute the <abs> from 0 - <n> when <n> < 0.
    Return an <OK: status> with <abs>.
}
```
How to compute the average of a list of:
`Reduce` with the `avg()` aggregator.

```aro
(AverageList: Example) {
    Create the <numbers> with [10, 20, 30, 40].
    Reduce the <avg: Float> from the <numbers> with avg().
    Return an <OK: status> with <avg>.
}
```
How to compute the difference of two set:
`Compute` with the `difference` qualifier.

```aro
(Diff: Example) {
    Create the <a> with [1, 2, 3].
    Create the <b> with [2, 4].
    Compute the <unique: difference> from <a> with <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <unique>.
}
```
How to compute the hash of a password?:
Use the `Compute` action with the `hash` qualifier.

```aro
(Hash Password: Example) {
    Compute the <hash: hash> from the <password>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <hash>.
}
```
How to compute the intersection of two s:
`Compute` with the `intersect` qualifier.

```aro
(Intersect: Example) {
    Create the <a> with [1, 2, 3].
    Create the <b> with [2, 3, 4].
    Compute the <common: intersect> from <a> with <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <common>.
}
```
How to compute the length of a string?:
Use the `Compute` action with the `length` qualifier.

```aro
(Measure: Example) {
    Compute the <len: length> from the <message>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <len>.
}
```
How to compute the max of a list of numb:
`Reduce` with `max()`.

```aro
(MaxList: Example) {
    Create the <numbers> with [4, 9, 2, 7].
    Reduce the <m: Integer> from the <numbers> with max().
    Return an <OK: status> with <m>.
}
```
How to compute the min of a list of numb:
`Reduce` with `min()`.

```aro
(MinList: Example) {
    Create the <numbers> with [4, 9, 2, 7].
    Reduce the <m: Integer> from the <numbers> with min().
    Return an <OK: status> with <m>.
}
```
How to compute the remainder of a divisi:
Use the `Compute` action with `%`.

```aro
(Remainder: Example) {
    Create the <a> with 17.
    Create the <b> with 5.
    Compute the <remainder> from <a> % <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <remainder>.
}
```
How to compute the square of a number?:
Multiply the number by itself.

```aro
(Square: Example) {
    Create the <n> with 7.
    Compute the <squared> from <n> * <n>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <squared>.
}
```
How to compute the sum of a list of numb:
`Reduce` with `sum()`.

```aro
(SumList: Example) {
    Create the <numbers> with [1, 2, 3].
    Reduce the <total: Integer> from the <numbers> with sum().
    Return an <OK: status> with <total>.
}
```
How to compute the union of two sets?:
Use the `union` qualifier with `Compute`.

```aro
(Combine: Example) {
    Compute the <combined: union> from the <set-a> and the <set-b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <combined>.
}
```
How to configure a runtime setting?:
Use the `Configure` action.

```aro
(SetTimeout: Example) {
    Configure the <http-client> with { timeout: 30 }.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <configure>.
}
```
How to configure the HTTP client timeout:
Use `Configure` against `<http-client>` before issuing the request.

```aro
(SlowRequest: Example) {
    Configure the <http-client> with { timeout: 60 }.
    Create the <api-url> with "https://example.com".
    Request the <response> from the <api-url>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <response>.
}
```
How to connect to a TCP socket?:
```aro
(ConnectSocket: Example) {
    Create the <addr> with { host: "127.0.0.1", port: 9000 }.
    Connect the <session> to the <socket-client> with <addr>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <session>.
}
```
How to convert a string to lowercase?:
Use the `Compute` action with the `lowercase` qualifier.

```aro
(Soften: Example) {
    Compute the <lower: lowercase> from the <message>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <lower>.
}
```
How to convert a string to uppercase?:
Use the `Compute` action with the `uppercase` qualifier.

```aro
(Shout: Example) {
    Compute the <upper: uppercase> from the <message>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <upper>.
}
```
How to copy a file from source to destin:
```aro
(CopyFile: Example) {
    Create the <src> with "in.txt".
    Create the <dst> with "out.txt".
    Copy the <file: src> to the <destination: dst>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <copy>.
}
```
How to count the items in a list?:
`Reduce` with `count()`.

```aro
(CountItems: Example) {
    Create the <items> with [1, 2, 3].
    Reduce the <n: Integer> from the <items> with count().
    Return an <OK: status> with <n>.
}
```
How to define a conditional branch with:
Append `when <cond>` to a statement to gate it.

```aro
(Guarded: Example) {
    Create the <count> with 5.
    Log "big" to the <console> when <count> > 3.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <check>.
}
```
How to define a user-defined action call:
Make the business activity `Action`. Then call as `Application.<Name>`.

```aro
(DoubleValue: Action takes <number>) {
    Extract the <n> from the <input: number>.
    Compute the <doubled> from <n> * 2.
    Return an <OK: status> with { doubled: <doubled> }.
}
```

Call site:

```aro
Application.DoubleValue the <res> from <5>.
```
How to delete a file?:
```aro
(DeleteFile: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "obsolete.txt".
    Delete the <file: path> for the <cleanup>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <deletion>.
}
```
How to delete an item from a repository?:
```aro
(DeleteItem: Example) {
    Create the <key> with 1.
    Delete the <item> from the <item-repository> where <id> is <key>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <deletion>.
}
```
How to divide two numbers?:
Use the `Compute` action with `/`.

```aro
(Divide: Example) {
    Compute the <quotient> from <a> / <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <quotient>.
}
```
How to emit a custom event?:
Use the `Emit` action with `<Name: event>`. Any feature set with `(... : Name Handler)` will fire.

```aro
(Process Order: Example) {
    Extract the <order> from the <input>.
    Emit an <OrderProcessed: event> with <order>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <order>.
}
```
How to expose Prometheus metrics?:
Configure the metrics endpoint and the HTTP server scrapes from `<metrics>` automatically.

```aro
(Application-Start: Service) {
    Configure the <metrics> with { path: "/metrics" }.
    Start the <http-server> with {}.
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to extract a field from an object?:
Use `Extract ... from <obj: field>`.

```aro
(ExtractField: Example) {
    Create the <user> with { name: "Ada", role: "admin" }.
    Extract the <name> from the <user: name>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <name>.
}
```
How to extract a path parameter from a r:
Use `Extract ... from <pathParameters: name>`.

```aro
(getOrder: Orders API) {
    Extract the <id> from the <pathParameters: id>.
    Retrieve the <order> from the <order-repository> where id = <id>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <order>.
}
```
How to extract a query parameter from a:
Use `Extract ... from <queryParameters: name>`.

```aro
(searchUsers: Users API) {
    Extract the <q> from the <queryParameters: q>.
    Retrieve the <users> from the <user-repository>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <users>.
}
```
How to extract a typed integer field fro:
Use `Extract the <name: Integer> from the <event: field>`. The type qualifier tells the runtime to parse the value as Integer (or fail loudly).

```aro
(Process Order: OrderCreated Handler) {
    Extract the <total: Integer> from the <event: total>.
    Log <total> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <total>.
}
```
How to extract a typed string field from:
```aro
(Greet User: UserSignedUp Handler) {
    Extract the <name: String> from the <event: name>.
    Log <name> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <name>.
}
```
How to extract the body of a request?:
Use `Extract ... from <request: body>`.

```aro
(createPost: Blog API) {
    Extract the <post> from the <request: body>.
    Store the <post> in the <post-repository>.
    Return a <Created: status> with <post>.
}
```
How to fetch a URL?:
Use `Request` against the `<http-client>`.

```aro
(FetchURL: Example) {
    Create the <url> with "https://example.com".
    Request the <response> from the <http-client> at <url>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <response>.
}
```
How to filter a list by a condition?:
Use a `For-each` with a `when` guard, or the `Filter` action if you only need the matching subset.

```aro
(Filter Adults: Example) {
    Filter the <adults> from the <people> where age > 17.
    Return an <OK: status> with <adults>.
}
```
How to format a date as a string?:
Use the `format` qualifier with a pattern string.

```aro
(FormatDate: Example) {
    Create the <today> with the <date: today>.
    Compute the <formatted: format> from <today> with "YYYY-MM-DD".
    Return an <OK: status> with <formatted>.
}
```
How to get file metadata like size and m:
Use the `Stat` action.

```aro
(FileStat: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "data.json".
    Stat the <info> from the <file: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <info>.
}
```
How to guard a handler so it only fires:
Append `when <field> == "value"` to the feature-set header.

```aro
(Ship Paid Order: OrderUpdated Handler) when <status> == "paid" {
    Extract the <order> from the <event: order>.
    Log <order> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <order>.
}
```
How to handle a GET route?:
Name the feature set after the operationId in `openapi.yaml` and let ARO dispatch the request.

```aro
(getUser: User API) {
    Extract the <id> from the <pathParameters: id>.
    Retrieve the <user> from the <user-repository> where id = <id>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <user>.
}
```
How to handle a POST route?:
Same naming convention as GET — the feature-set name matches the operationId. Use `Extract` to pull the body.

```aro
(createUser: User API) {
    Extract the <data> from the <request: body>.
    Create the <user> with <data>.
    Emit a <UserCreated: event> with <user>.
    Return a <Created: status> with <user>.
}
```
How to handle a custom event?:
Name the feature set `(name: <EventName> Handler)`.

```aro
(Send Email: OrderProcessed Handler) {
    Extract the <order> from the <event: order>.
    Send the <confirmation> to the <order: email>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <notification>.
}
```
How to increment a Prometheus counter me:
Use `Log` with a `<metric: name>` target — the runtime exposes registered metrics at `/metrics` when the HTTP server is up.

```aro
(handleRequest: API) {
    Log 1 to the <metric: requests-total>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <request>.
}
```
How to invoke a user-defined action?:
Call it as `Application.<Name>` from any other feature set.

```aro
(Caller: Example) {
    Application.DoubleValue the <result> from 21.
    Extract the <doubled> from the <result: doubled>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <doubled>.
}
```
How to keep an application alive to proc:
Use the `Keepalive` action.

```aro
(Application-Start: Server) {
    Start the <http-server> with <contract>.
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to list files in a directory?:
Use the `List` action against `<directory: path>`.

```aro
(ListDir: Example) {
    Create the <path> with ".".
    List the <entries> from the <directory: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <entries>.
}
```
How to log a message to the console?:
Use the `Log` action with the `<console>` system object.

```aro
(Greeting: Example) {
    Log "Hello, World!" to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <message>.
}
```
How to log structured data?:
Log an object literal; the formatter renders it per the active context.

```aro
(LogStructured: Example) {
    Create the <event> with { kind: "login", user: "ada" }.
    Log <event> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <log>.
}
```
How to log to a metrics counter?:
Use the `Log` action with a `<metric>` target.

```aro
(IncCounter: Example) {
    Log 1 to the <metric: requests-total>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <count>.
}
```
How to merge two collections?:
Use the `Merge` action.

```aro
(MergeLists: Example) {
    Create the <left> with [1, 2].
    Create the <right> with [3, 4].
    Merge the <combined> from <left> with <right>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <combined>.
}
```
How to move a file?:
```aro
(MoveFile: Example) {
    Create the <src> with "in.txt".
    Create the <dst> with "out.txt".
    Move the <file: src> to the <destination: dst>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <move>.
}
```
How to multiply two numbers?:
Use the `Compute` action with `*`.

```aro
(Multiply: Example) {
    Compute the <product> from <a> * <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <product>.
}
```
How to observe changes on a repository?:
Name the feature set `(name: <repo> Observer)` and Extract from `<event: …>`.

```aro
(Audit Items: item-repository Observer) {
    Extract the <item> from the <event: item>.
    Log <item> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <audit>.
}
```
How to parse YAML?:
```aro
(ReadYAML: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "config.yaml".
    Read the <config> from the <file: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <config>.
}
```
How to parse a date from a string?:
Use `Parse` against `<date: ...>`.

```aro
(ParseDate: Example) {
    Create the <text> with "2026-05-23".
    Parse the <when> from the <date: text>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <when>.
}
```
How to publish a variable across feature:
Use the `Publish` action — other feature sets can then read the published variable.

```aro
(PublishGreeting: Example) {
    Create the <greeting> with "Hello".
    Publish as <welcome> <greeting>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <greeting>.
}
```
How to publish a variable so other featu:
Use the `Publish as <alias> <variable>.` form. Other feature sets in the same business activity scope can then read `<alias>` directly.

```aro
(Configure App: Application-Start) {
    Create the <base-url> with "https://api.example.com".
    Publish as <api-base> <base-url>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <configure>.
}
```
How to read JSON from a file?:
```aro
(ReadJSON: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "data.json".
    Read the <data> from the <file: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <data>.
}
```
How to read a CSV file?:
Format-aware I/O (ARO-0040): just `Read` the file; the runtime parses by extension.

```aro
(ReadCSV: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "data.csv".
    Read the <rows> from the <file: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <rows>.
}
```
How to read a file in ARO?:
Use the `Read` action against the `<file-system>` system object.

```aro
(Read Config: Example) {
    Read the <content> from the <file-system> at "config.yaml".
    Return an <OK: status> with <content>.
}
```
How to read command line parameters?:
Use the `Parameters` action.

```aro
(ReadArgs: Example) {
    Parameters the <args> for the <application>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <args>.
}
```
How to read command-line parameters in a:
Use the `Parameters` action.

```aro
(Application-Start: CLI Tool) {
    Parameters the <args> for the <application>.
    Log <args> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to read environment variables?:
Use `Extract ... from <environment: NAME>`.

```aro
(ReadEnv: Example) {
    Extract the <home> from the <environment: HOME>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <home>.
}
```
How to read from stdin in ARO?:
Pipe source through `aro` and the engine reads it as a program; for runtime stdin, use the stdin system object.

```aro
(ReadStdin: Example) {
    Read the <line> from the <stdin>.
    Log <line> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <line>.
}
```
How to render a Mustache template with a:
```aro
(RenderGreeting: Example) {
    Create the <template> with "Hello, {{name}}!".
    Create the <data> with { name: "Ada" }.
    Render the <html> from the <template> with <data>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <html>.
}
```
How to render a template?:
Use the `Render` action against a Mustache-style template string.

```aro
(RenderGreeting: Example) {
    Create the <template> with "Hello, {{name}}!".
    Create the <data> with { name: "Ada" }.
    Render the <html> from the <template> with <data>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <html>.
}
```
How to retrieve an item from a repositor:
```aro
(GetItem: Example) {
    Create the <key> with 1.
    Retrieve the <item> from the <item-repository> where <id> is <key>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <item>.
}
```
How to retry a failed external call?:
Use `Configure` to set retry behaviour, then call as usual.

```aro
(RetryFetch: Example) {
    Configure the <http-client> with { retries: 3, backoff: "exponential" }.
    Create the <url> with "https://example.com".
    Request the <response> from the <http-client> at <url>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <response>.
}
```
How to reverse a list?:
Use the `reverse` qualifier on `Compute`.

```aro
(ReverseList: Example) {
    Create the <items> with [1, 2, 3].
    Compute the <reversed: reverse> from <items>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <reversed>.
}
```
How to round a number?:
Use the `round` qualifier.

```aro
(Round: Example) {
    Create the <x> with 3.7.
    Compute the <r: round> from <x>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <r>.
}
```
How to run a TCP socket server?:
```aro
(Application-Start: Echo Server) {
    Start the <socket-server> with { port: 9000 }.
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to schedule a recurring task?:
Use the `Schedule` action.

```aro
(Tick: Example) {
    Schedule the <tick> for the <events> with "every 5 minutes".
    Return an <OK: status> for the <schedule>.
}
```
How to send a message over a WebSocket?:
```aro
(BroadcastChat: Example) {
    Create the <message> with { kind: "chat", text: "hi" }.
    Send the <message> to the <websocket: clients>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <broadcast>.
}
```
How to send a typed body to an HTTP POST:
Use `Request the <response> from the <api-url> with <payload>.` — the runtime serializes the body per the URL's path schema.

```aro
(PostJSON: Example) {
    Create the <api-url> with "https://example.com/api".
    Create the <payload> with { name: "Ada", role: "admin" }.
    Request the <response> from the <api-url> with <payload>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <response>.
}
```
How to send an HTTP POST request?:
`Request` with method POST and a body.

```aro
(PostJSON: Example) {
    Create the <url> with "https://example.com/api".
    Create the <payload> with { name: "Ada" }.
    Request the <response> from the <http-client> at <url> with <payload>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <response>.
}
```
How to sort a list?:
Use the `sort` qualifier on `Compute`.

```aro
(SortList: Example) {
    Create the <items> with [3, 1, 2].
    Compute the <sorted: sort> from <items>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <sorted>.
}
```
How to stage files before committing?:
Use `Stage ... to the <git>`.

```aro
(Stage All: Example) {
    Stage the <files> to the <git> with ".".
    Return an <OK: status> with <files>.
}
```
How to start an HTTP server?:
Define `Application-Start` and use the `Start` action with the `<http-server>` system object. The server reads routes from `openapi.yaml`.

```aro
(Application-Start: My API) {
    Start the <http-server> with <contract>.
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to stop an HTTP server gracefully?:
Put `Stop the <http-server>` in an Application-End handler.

```aro
(Application-End: Success) {
    Stop the <http-server> with <application>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <shutdown>.
}
```
How to store an item in a repository?:
```aro
(SaveItem: Example) {
    Create the <item> with { id: 1, name: "Ada" }.
    Store the <item> into the <item-repository>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <item>.
}
```
How to subtract two numbers?:
Use the `Compute` action with `-`.

```aro
(Subtract: Example) {
    Compute the <difference> from <a> - <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <difference>.
}
```
How to sum two numbers?:
Use the `Compute` action with a `+` expression. Both operands must be `<bracketed>` variables or literals.

```aro
(Sum Numbers: Example) {
    Compute the <sum> from <a> + <b>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <sum>.
}
```

Note: `Compute` uses the `from` preposition, and there is no `<request>` because this is a plain action, not an HTTP handler.
How to update an item in a repository?:
Re-store the modified record; Store upserts by id.

```aro
(UpdateItem: Example) {
    Create the <item> with { id: 1, name: "Ada Lovelace" }.
    Store the <item> into the <item-repository>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <item>.
}
```
How to use a plugin qualifier from the C:
Plugin qualifiers are namespaced as `handle.qualifier`. The handle is declared in `plugin.yaml` (PascalCase).

```aro
(PickRandom: Example) {
    Create the <items> with ["a", "b", "c"].
    Compute the <pick: Collections.pick-random> from <items>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <pick>.
}
```
How to use a plugin qualifier?:
Plugin qualifiers are namespaced — call as `<value: handle.qualifier>`.

```aro
(PickRandom: Example) {
    Create the <items> with ["a", "b", "c"].
    Compute the <pick: collections.pick-random> from <items>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <pick>.
}
```
How to use a state machine in ARO?:
Use `Accept` to consume a transition; the runtime tracks state per record.

```aro
(Place Order: Orders) {
    Extract the <order> from the <input>.
    Accept the <transition> for the <order> with "placed".
    Return an <OK: status> with <order>.
}
```
How to use a store file for read-only se:
Drop a `.store` file in the application root. The runtime auto-loads it as a repository named after the basename (`products.store` → `<products-repository>`). Read-only unless the file has `chmod o+w`.

```aro
(listProducts: Catalog) {
    Retrieve the <products> from the <products-repository>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <products>.
}
```

File layout:
```
MyCatalog/
├── openapi.yaml
├── main.aro
└── products.store    # YAML seed list — read-only at runtime
```
How to use a store file for seed data?: Drop a `.store` file in the application directory; the runtime auto-loads it as a repository (`products.store` → `<products-repository>`). Read-only unless the file has `chmod o+w`.
How to use git status in ARO?:
Use `Retrieve ... from the <git>`.

```aro
(Show Status: Example) {
    Retrieve the <status> from the <git>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <status>.
}
```
How to use sink syntax to put an express:
Put the expression directly where a bound `<result>` would normally go. Sink syntax (ARO-0043) computes inline without naming.

```aro
(Sink: Example) {
    Create the <a> with 3.
    Create the <b> with 4.
    Return an <OK: status> with <a> + <b>.
}
```
How to validate input data?:
Use a `when` guard to Throw on bad input.

```aro
(ValidateInput: Example) {
    Create the <name> with "".
    Throw a <BadRequest: status> with "name is required" when <name> == "".
    Return an <OK: status> with <name>.
}
```
How to watch a directory for file change:
Start the `<file-monitor>` and add a `File Event Handler` feature set to react.

```aro
(Application-Start: Watcher) {
    Start the <file-monitor> with { directory: "." }.
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to write JSON to a file?:
```aro
(WriteJSON: Example) {
    Create the <path> with "data.json".
    Create the <data> with { id: 1, name: "Ada" }.
    Write the <data> to the <file: path>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <write>.
}
```
How to write a hello world application i:
An ARO application needs exactly one `Application-Start` feature set.

```aro
(Application-Start: Hello World) {
    Log "Hello, World!" to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to write an Application-End handler:
```aro
(Application-End: Success) {
    Log "shutting down" to the <console>.
    Stop the <http-server> with <application>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <shutdown>.
}
```
How to write an Application-Start featur:
Exactly one per application. It's the entry point.

```aro
(Application-Start: My App) {
    Log "Starting..." to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}
```
How to write to a file in ARO?:
Use the `Write` action against the `<file-system>` system object.

```aro
(Save Report: Example) {
    Write the <report> to the <file-system> at "out/report.txt".
    Return an <OK: status> for the <write>.
}
```
I want to learn ARO. Where should I star: Start with `Examples/HelloWorld/main.aro` — it's the smallest valid program. Then `Examples/Calculator` (arithmetic and `Compute`), `Examples/UserService` (full HTTP API with repositories), and `Examples/EventExample` (event-driven flow). The Book chapter is at `Book/TheLanguageGuide/`.
I'm new to ARO. What do I need to know f: Three things. (1) Every statement is `Verb [the] <result> preposition [the] <object>.` ending in a period. (2) Variables are immutable — use a new name for a derived value. (3) Every feature set lives inside `(Name: Activity) { ... }` and ends with `Return` or `Throw`. After that, the rest is action vocabulary.
Idiomatic ARO for: \"compute SHA256 of a:
```aro
(HashAndStore: Example) {
    Create the <password> with "s3cret".
    Compute the <hash: hash> from <password>.
    Store the <hash> into the <password-repository>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <hash>.
}
```
Idiomatic ARO for: \"deduplicate a list:
Group by id then take the last record of each group.

```aro
(Dedup: Example) {
    Create the <records> with [{ id: 1, v: "old" }, { id: 1, v: "new" }].
    Group the <by-id> from <records> by id.
    Return an <OK: status> with <by-id>.
}
```
Idiomatic ARO for: \"every 5 minutes, fe:
```aro
(Application-Start: Weather Poller) {
    Schedule the <tick> for the <events> with "every 5 minutes".
    Keepalive the <application> for the <events>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <startup>.
}

(Poll Weather: tick Handler) {
    Create the <url> with "https://api.example.com/weather".
    Request the <weather> from the <http-client> at <url>.
    Extract the <temp> from the <weather: temperature>.
    Log <temp> to the <console>.
    Return an <OK: status> with <temp>.
}
```
Insert a Compute step that doubles a val: Between `Extract the <n> from ...` and the `Return`, add `Compute the <doubled> from <n> * 2.` and pass `<doubled>` to the Return.
Is ARO compiled or interpreted?: Both. `aro run` interprets directly; `aro build` compiles to a native binary via LLVM IR. The runtime libraries (HTTP server, file system, sockets, git via libgit2) are linked in either way.
Is there a REPL for ARO?: Yes — `aro repl` starts an interactive session that compiles and executes one statement at a time, with history.
Extract, Compute, Parse, Retrieve, Fetch, Pull, Clone, Read, Request, Validate, Compare, Create, Transform, Stage, Checkout, Group, Merge, Filter, Reduce, Render, Sort, Reverse, Return, Throw, Publish, Store, Log, Send, Emit, Commit, Push, Tag, Write, Append, Copy, Move, Delete, Exists, Stat, List, Start, Stop, Keepalive, Configure, Accept, Schedule, Require, Connect.
Make this feature set publish its result: Add a `Publish as <alias> <variable>.` at the end. Other feature sets read it as `<alias>` from any scope.
Quick sanity check: can I have two `Appl: No. Exactly one `Application-Start` per application — the compiler errors out on zero or more than one.
Quick sanity check: does ARO have `if/el: Not as a keyword. Use a `when` guard on a single statement (`Log "x" to <console> when <cond>.`) or a `match` block for multi-way branching.
Quick sanity check: is `Log <text> to th: Yes — that's a complete `Log` statement inside a feature set. Just make sure the surrounding `(Name: Activity) { ... }` wrapper is present and `<text>` is bound somewhere earlier.
`echo 'Log "Hi" to the <console>.' | aro` — stdin-piped one-liner, no file needed.
Refactor this ARO code to use the qualif: `Compute the <len: length> from the <message>.` — the qualifier names the operation, and `<len>` names the result so you can have multiple lengths in the same scope.
Refactor: extract the Compute into its o: Pull the Compute into a `(Doubler: Action takes <number>) { ... }` feature set, then replace the original Compute with `Application.Doubler the <result> from <input>.` plus an Extract for the returned field.

Pull the Compute into a `(Doubler: Action takes <number>) { ... }` feature set, then replace the original Compute with `Application.Doubler the <result> from <input>.` plus an Extract for the returned field.
Remove the unused Log statement from thi: Delete the Log line. ARO doesn't require a Log to keep a binding alive — bindings flow through subsequent statements regardless.
Rename `<sum>` to `<total>` consistently: Search/replace `<sum>` → `<total>` across the file. ARO's immutability means there are no aliases to worry about — every reference is to the same binding.
Rewrite this feature set so the variable: Replace any camelCase/PascalCase/snake_case names with hyphenated lowercase: `<userName>` → `<user-name>`, `<order_id>` → `<order-id>`, `<TotalAmount>` → `<total-amount>`.

Split this 30-line feature set into two:
Identify the boundary (usually validation→persistence→notification). Emit a custom event at the boundary and create a Handler feature set for the second half. Example:

```aro
(createOrder: API) {
    Extract the <order> from the <request: body>.
    Store the <order> into the <order-repository>.
    Emit an <OrderCreated: event> with <order>.
    Return a <Created: status> with <order>.
}

(Notify: OrderCreated Handler) {
    Extract the <order> from the <event: order>.
    Send the <confirmation> to the <order: email>.
    Return an <OK: status> for the <notification>.
}
```
Summarize the contract-first HTTP model: Routes are defined in `openapi.yaml`, not in code. The runtime starts an HTTP server only if `openapi.yaml` exists; for each `operationId` it expects a feature set named `(operationId: API Name) { ... }`. Path/query parameters and request bodies are extracted via `<pathParameters: id>`, `<queryParameters: q>`, `<request: body>`. No openapi.yaml = no server.
Tell me about ARO's qualifier-as-name sy:
When you need two results of the same operation, the qualifier names the operation and the variable name is on the left:

```aro
Compute the <first-len: length> from the <first>.
Compute the <second-len: length> from the <second>.
```

This avoids the ambiguity of `Compute the <length>` (is `length` the operation or the variable?) and lets both results coexist in the same scope.
ARO is a DSL where every statement is `Verb [the] <result> preposition [the] <object>.`. Programs are directories of `.aro` files plus an optional `openapi.yaml` (for HTTP). `Application-Start` is the entry point. Variables are immutable. Actions are lazy by default; effects keep source order. Errors are the failing statement rendered as a sentence. Compile to native via `aro build`, interpret via `aro run`, test via `aro test`. Extend with Swift/Rust/C/Python plugins.
The compiler complains \"is-valid\" is a: ARO forbids the `is-`, `with-`, and `empty-` prefixes for variable names (they read like adjectives, not nouns, and conflict with `is` in where-clauses). Rename to a noun form: `<validity>`, `<valid-flag>`, or `<validated>`.
The model wrote `Compute the <total> for: `Compute` takes `from`, not `for`. Fixed: `Compute the <total> from the <items>` — but Compute also needs an operation, so probably you wanted `Reduce` instead: `Reduce the <total: Integer> from the <items> with sum().`
Translate this JSON config schema into a: Paste the schema. The generic shape is: `Read the <config> from the <file: "config.json">.` followed by `Extract` per field per the schema.
Translate this Python snippet to ARO: `t:
```aro
(WithTax: Example) {
    Create the <prices> with [19.99, 24.50, 9.95].
    Reduce the <sub: Float> from the <prices> with sum().
    Compute the <total> from <sub> * 1.19.
    Return an <OK: status> with <total>.
}
```
Walk through the lifecycle of an event i: An event is produced by `Emit a <Name: event> with <payload>`. The runtime's `EventBus` finds every feature set named `(... : Name Handler)` and invokes each with `<event: …>` bound to the payload. Handlers run independently; the emitter doesn't wait. State updates flow back through repositories rather than direct returns.
What does \"Sink syntax\" mean (ARO-0043: Sink syntax (ARO-0043) lets you put an expression in the *result* position instead of a binding name. The expression is evaluated and its value flows on. Useful when you want to compute and immediately consume without naming the intermediate.
What does the Keepalive action do?: `Keepalive` blocks the calling feature set until the runtime receives a shutdown signal (SIGINT/SIGTERM). Used in `Application-Start` to keep an event-driven app (HTTP server, file watcher, socket server) running so the event loop can dispatch incoming events. Without it the Start action returns and the process exits.
What is ARO?: ARO (Action Result Object) is a DSL for writing business logic as natural-language statements. The runtime compiles it to native code via LLVM, dispatches HTTP routes via OpenAPI, persists state via repositories, and treats every error as a sentence ("Cannot retrieve the user from the user-repository where id = 530") instead of a stack trace.
What makes ARO different from other DSLs: It reads like English; every statement is `Verb [the] <result> preposition [the] <object>.`. The runtime turns errors back into the failing statement verbatim ("Cannot retrieve the user from the user-repository where id = 530"). Routes come from `openapi.yaml`, not annotations. Variables are immutable. Lazy execution is default and effects keep source order.
What's ARO-0080?: Git Actions. A `<git>` system object backed by libgit2 — `Retrieve` for status/log/branch, `Stage`/`Commit`/`Push`/`Pull`/`Clone`/`Checkout`/`Tag`. Each emits a corresponding event.
What's ARO-0081?: User-Defined Actions. A feature set whose business activity is `Action` becomes callable application-wide as `Application.<Name>`. The `takes <arg>` slot is sugar for a single positional argument extracted as `input.<arg>`.
What's the role classification of `Extra: OWN role — Extract works on already-internal data: pulling a field from an object, or extracting from a structured input like `<request: body>` that the runtime already materialised.
What's wrong with: `Compute the <total>: Every ARO statement ends with a period. Add it: `Compute the <total> from <price> * <qty>.`
Where do I download ARO?: macOS: `brew tap arolang/aro && brew install aro`. Linux: download from the latest release at https://github.com/arolang/aro/releases or build from source with `swift build -c release`.
Which built-in actions are EXPORT role?: Internal → External: Publish, Store, Log, Send, Emit, Commit, Push, Tag, Stage, Write.
Which built-in actions are REQUEST role?: External → Internal: Extract (from structured input), Parse, Retrieve, Fetch, Pull, Clone, Read, Request.
Why does every feature set need a Return: Every feature set produces a status object that the runtime hands back to whatever invoked it (HTTP response, event ack, action result). `Return` produces a success path; `Throw` produces a failure path with a status code. The compiler rejects feature sets that fall off the end — there's no implicit "nothing" status, because every caller needs to know what happened.
Why is `<console>` written with angle br: `<console>` is a system object — same syntax as every other identifier in ARO. The angle brackets mark it as a referenceable name (vs. a literal). Bare `Console` would be parsed as an identifier expecting a binding, not a system reference. Consistency: every variable and system object uses `<lowercase>` form.
Why is `String concatenation: ++` (not `: `+` is reserved for numeric addition. `++` is the string-concatenation operator, distinct so `"1" + "2"` doesn't silently produce `"12"` when you meant `3`. Use `<left> ++ <right>` for strings.
Why would I use ARO over plain Python or: When the domain is business logic that you want a non-engineer to read. The trade-off: ARO's grammar is rigid (you can't sneak in clever code), but reviews go faster and the error messages are sentences instead of stack traces.
